description: Figure 2 presents the aging hardening responses of three Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels—15Al, 17Al, and 19Al—showing Vickers hardness evolution as a function of aging time (0–10 h) at temperatures from 525 °C to 650 °C. For each alloy, the left panel displays hardness-time curves at six discrete aging temperatures, while the right panel provides a corresponding contour plot mapping hardness across the temperature-time parameter space. All alloys exhibit rapid hardness increases within the first hour, followed by plateau or slight over-aging behavior. The 15Al alloy achieves the highest peak hardness of approximately 966 HV at 600 °C for 6 h, whereas the 17Al and 19Al alloys peak at about 925 HV (575 °C/6 h) and 918 HV (600 °C/10 h), respectively. Notably, the optimal aging temperature window shifts to higher temperatures as Al content increases, and the 19Al alloy shows markedly slower hardening kinetics at lower aging temperatures compared with 15Al. These results demonstrate that increasing Al content retards the aging hardening response and reduces the peak hardness in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu steels, consistent with delayed β-Mn precipitation and transformation kinetics.
